Experiment_description: Preliminary implementation and comparison of Error-Taxonomy-Guided Curriculum Learning (ETCL) versus baseline uniform training for synthetic theorem proving tasks. Multiple nodes explored different implementations of a transformer-based proof generation model trained on synthetic theorems with simulated verifiers that categorize errors into types (type mismatch, missing hypothesis, wrong tactic, scope violation).
Significance: These experiments establish initial baselines for comparing curriculum learning approaches in theorem proving. The findings reveal important trade-offs: while ETCL can achieve lower training loss, it tends to exhibit worse generalization (higher validation loss) compared to uniform baseline training. The conflicting results across implementations highlight the sensitivity of curriculum learning methods to task difficulty and evaluation criteria, which is crucial for future experimental design.
Description: The experiments implemented simplified simulations of the ETCL framework for theorem proving without actual Lean 4 verification. Key components included: (1) synthetic datasets of theorems with ground-truth proofs, (2) simulated verifiers categorizing errors into taxonomic types, (3) small transformer models for proof generation, and (4) curriculum strategies that progressively include harder error categories. Two distinct implementation approaches were tested - one with graduated verification thresholds and intermediate metrics (token accuracy, similarity), and another focusing on fixing teacher forcing alignment and autoregressive generation bugs. Both compared baseline (uniform training) against ETCL across multiple epochs.

Key_numerical_results.description: Best proof success rate for baseline (uniform training) in the more discriminative experimental setup
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The baseline achieved 66% best proof success rate compared to ETCL's 56%, suggesting uniform training provides better generalization on this synthetic task. The 10 percentage point gap indicates curriculum learning may not always be beneficial.

Key_numerical_results.description: Final validation loss for baseline in discriminative setup
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Baseline validation loss (1.09) was lower than ETCL's (1.48), indicating better generalization despite similar training losses (~0.02). This gap of 0.38 in validation loss suggests ETCL overfits to the curriculum structure.

Key_numerical_results.description: Proof success rate for baseline in the easier synthetic task setup
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Perfect 100% success rate suggests the synthetic task in three of the four nodes was too easy to discriminate between methods, highlighting the importance of task difficulty calibration in curriculum learning experiments.

Key_numerical_results.description: Final training loss for ETCL in easier setup
Key_numerical_results.analysis: ETCL achieved much lower training loss (0.29) compared to baseline (1.00), yet had higher validation loss (1.87 vs 1.01), demonstrating a classic overfitting pattern where curriculum-based training fits the training distribution too tightly.

Key_numerical_results.description: AUC of proof success rate over training for baseline
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The area under the curve metric captures cumulative learning efficiency. Baseline's AUC (23.9) significantly exceeded ETCL's (17.5), indicating faster and more consistent learning progress throughout training.
